coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly of graphene quantum dots into high-aspect-ratio carbon fibers for effective solar water desalination
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Tengfei
Guo, Chun
Qian, Sheng
Wang, Junhua
Zhu, Kun
Xue, Huaiguo
Tian, Jingqi
coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven seawater desalinization has been regarded as a sustainable technology for the production of fresh water with solar-thermal energy conversion, wherein the photothermal conversion materials are crucial to achieve high efficiency. Herein, we demonstrate the self-assembly of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) into high-aspect-ratio carbon nanofibers (CFs), which further twine into deformable foam as solar absorbers. The following phosphidation further converts the CFs into phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs), which possess narrowed band-gap, interconnected crystal/amorphous domains within the fibers with a reconstructed fraction of localized/isolated sp2 domains, favorable the localization of converted heat into small region and minimized heat radiation loss. All these structure and composition advantages endow the PNCFs-based foam inspiring photothermal performance, including a high water production rate of 1.5 kg m−2 h−1 with an external solar-thermal energy conversion efficiency of 93.92 % under one sun irradiation intensity, which suggests a high efficiency, reusability, and long-term stability of PNCFs-based solar desalination system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Self-assembly
Nanofiber
Long-aspect ratio
Solar steam generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Self-assembly of NGQDs into carbon nanofibers (CFs)
Synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs) and nitrogen doped carbon nanofibers (NCFs)
Synthesis and characterization of NGQDs and CFs
Synthesis and characterization of NCFs and PNCFs
Photothermal seawater evaporation performance of CFs-, NCFs-, and PNCFs-based foams

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22102140 , the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20211602 ), the Qing Lan Project of Yangzhou University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image with size distribution (inset) and (b) HRTEM with PL image (inset) of NGQDs. (c) Low- and (d) high-magnification SEM images of CFs. (e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of CFs. (g) STEM and element mapping images of single fiber. (h) XRD pattern of CFs.
XPS analysis of CFs: (a) the survey spectrum, high-resolution spectrum of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s. (e) Raman and (f) FT-IR spectrum of NGQDs and CFs.
(a) Schematic illustration of the self-assembly of NGQDs. Fluorescence confocal images (b–d) and AFM images (e–g) recorded at different assembly period: 0 h, 36 h, and 72 h.

(a, b) SEM image of PNCFs. (c) TEM and (d) HRTEM image of PNCFs. (e) STEM image and selective area EDX elemental mappings of PNCFs. (f) XRD pattern, (g) Raman pattern and (g) line-scanning EDX of PNCFs.

(a) Schematic illustration of the evaporator with the PNCFs foam as the solar absorber. (b) Optical image of the CFs, NCFs and PNCFs foam. (c) Time-lapse snapshots of absorption of a water droplet by the PNCFs foam. (d) Absorption spectra of the CFs, NCFs and PNCFs. (e) Measured temperature variation profiles of the CFs, NCFs and PNCFs foams under one sun irradiation. Inset: Infrared thermal images of corresponding samples at the stable temperature. (f) Evaporation rates and (g) energy conversion efficiencies of the PNCFs foam compared with other carbon-based solar absorbers. (h) The measured concentrations of Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and K+ in an artificial seawater before and after desalination. The dashed line presents the drinkable water standard proposed by WHO. (i) Reusability of CFs, NCFs and PNCFs foam under one sun irradiation over 10 cycles. (j) Mechanistic illustration of the superior photothermal performance of the PNCFs.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly of graphene quantum dots into high-aspect-ratio carbon fibers for effective solar water desalination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, 180 Si-Wang-Ting Road, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, 180 Si-Wang-Ting Road, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-assembly of graphene quantum dots into long-aspect-ratio carbon fibers with further phosphidation could generate the formation of phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon fibers based deformable foam as solar absorber, which displays an external solar-thermal energy conversion efficiency up to 94 % under one sun irradiation intensity, a high water production rate of 1.5 kg m−2 h−1 remained no obvious degradation for 3 days.

Solar-driven seawater desalinization has been regarded as a sustainable technology for the production of fresh water with solar-thermal energy conversion, wherein the photothermal conversion materials are crucial to achieve high efficiency. Herein, we demonstrate the self-assembly of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) into high-aspect-ratio carbon nanofibers (CFs), which further twine into deformable foam as solar absorbers. The following phosphidation further converts the CFs into phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs), which possess narrowed band-gap, interconnected crystal/amorphous domains within the fibers with a reconstructed fraction of localized/isolated sp2 domains, favorable the localization of converted heat into small region and minimized heat radiation loss. All these structure and composition advantages endow the PNCFs-based foam inspiring photothermal performance, including a high water production rate of 1.5 kg m−2 h−1 with an external solar-thermal energy conversion efficiency of 93.92 % under one sun irradiation intensity, which suggests a high efficiency, reusability, and long-term stability of PNCFs-based solar desalination system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The worldwide increase in water pollution and freshwater demand appeals to energy-saving and sustainable approaches for clean water production. Desalination of seawater has emerged as one of the most promising techniques owing to the abundance of seawater on the earth [1]. Compared with the traditional membrane-based filtration [2], and thermal-based multi-stage flash process which would cause huge energy consumption with severe environmental pollution [3], the recently developed solar-driven seawater desalinization system has been regarded as an inexhaustible environmentally friendly and sustainable technology, attracting much research focus owing to the direct convert solar energy into thermal energy to drive water evaporation. With the photothermal conversion materials floating at the air–water interface, such a method can effectively harvest the solar energy and convert it into heat with decreased heat loss to the bulk water, thereby increasing the photothermal conversion efficiency, opening a new avenue for sustainable fresh water supply [4].

The key to efficient solar steam evaporation is the photothermal conversion material. Considering the characteristics for efficient photothermal conversion, carbonaceous materials with low toxicity, efficient light absorbing ability across the entire solar spectrum (300–2500 nm), porous structure, high mechanical strength, as well as abundant origin have been intensively studied as cost-effective and safe candidate for large-scale solar-driven seawater desalinization over the past decade [5–10], such as amorphous carbons [6,7], exfoliated graphite [8], graphene [9], and carbon nanotube [10], etc. It is commonly accepted that the wide absorption of light origins from the optical transition of their π-band. The subsequent light-to-heat conversion efficiency has been demonstrated to be influenced by mainly two critical factors: one is the low photothermal conductivity of photothermal conversion materials to localize the converted heat into dispersed areas around the photothermal material without heating the bulk water, suppressing thermal conduction away from the hot internal region; another is the emissivity induced heat radiation loss, which can be minimized in large crystalline domains but inevitably due to the multiple scattering centers in amorphous materials [11,12]. Amorphous carbons aare usually featured with low thermal conductivity while beset by low light-to-heat conversion efficiency, whereas graphitic carbons with high crystallinity show the reverse trend [11,12], suggesting that carbonaceous materials consisting of both amorphous and crystal domains would be promising photothermal materials, which requires rational structure design.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess atomically-thin planar sp2 plane with peripheral functional groups, and variable band-gap (separation between π and π* orbitals) for light absorption by varying size, grafting chemical moieties or doping heteroatoms [13]. Although GQDs have been used in photothermal therapy and imaging areas, their application in solar desalination is still plagued by inherent disadvantages, mainly from the small size and high dispersibility caused difficulty in the manufacture of robust and self-supported architectures for solar absorption and conversion [14–17]. Integration with robust substrate such as graphene and PU could improve the mechanical characteristics, but also cause other issues such as the inevitable hinderance for mass and heat transfer at the hetero-interface. Previous efforts in GQD-assemblies only achieve limited success in fibers [18], tubes [19], films [17,20], and polymorphs [21]. Generally, they required GQDs with pre-modifications such as alkyl chains decoration [22,23], or the assistance of template/surfactant [24], allowed little or no external control over self-assembly itself and yielded fixed architectures, and hinder the potential application of the GQD-assemblies. It is quite challenging to design GQD-based assemblies for photothermal applications.

Herein, we demonstrate the self-assembly of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) into supramolecular carbon nanofibers (CFs) with long-aspect-ratio, which further were converted into phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs) and twined into foam-like solar absorber. Featured with graphitic domain surrounded by peripheral functional groups, the assembly of NGQDs is driven by π-stacking and hydrogen bonding, leading to the formation of unique interconnected crystal and amorphous domains in PNCFs. Twining such PNCFs into foam induces the favorable hierarchical with mesoporous and macroporous open cells. Furthermore, the band-gap could be regulated by the incorporation of N and P heteroatoms in the PNCFs. Notably, such PNCFs-based foam exhibits an external solar-thermal energy conversion efficiency for solar desalination up to 93.92 % under one sun irradiation intensity with water production rate of 1.5 kg m−2 h−1 remained no obvious degradation for 3 days, indicating the high efficiency, reusability, and long-term stability of PNCFs-based solar desalination system.

Pyrene was purchased from Mackin Biochemcial Co. Ltd (Shanghai). Urea, HNO3, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, and MgCl2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. NaH2PO2 was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pte. Ltd. (Singapore). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified using a Millipore system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 1.0 g of pyrene and 80 mL of nitric acid were mixed in a 250 mL round bottom flask under refluxing at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain a yellow suspension. The cooled mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dried to obtain 1,3,6-trinitropyrene. Then 320 mg of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 298 mg of urea were dispersed in 60 ml of water under sonication for 2 h and transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave, followed with the hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained purple suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and then transferred to a dialysis bag (MW 3500) for dialysis to remove sodium salt and unfused small molecules. After then, the dispersion was dried at 80 °C to obtain dark-brown NGQD powder for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembly of NGQDs into carbon nanofibers (CFs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 6 mg of the NGQD powder was dispersed in water (60 mL) followed by continuous stirring for 10 min. Then the dispersion was kept in dark at room temperature for assembly. After 72 h, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with water several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs) and nitrogen doped carbon nanofibers (NCFs)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, NaH2PO2 and CFs (the molar ratio of C to P is 1:10) were placed in a tube furnace under argon, which was heated to 400 °C at a ramping rate of 2 °C min−1 and held for 60 min. For comparison, NCFs were prepared with the same procedure in the absence of NaH2PO2.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterizations were performed on a Bruker D8 Advance with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). A field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss_Supra55) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN operated at 300 kV) were used for morphology and structure characterizations. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi using Mg as the excitation source. Contact angle measurements were carried out by Data physics OCA20. The diffuse reflective spectroscopy was recorded with Cary 5000 UV–Vis-NIR Absorption Spectrometer UV. Raman spectra were recorded with In Via Laser Confocal Raman Spectrometer (INVIA REFLEX). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with Cary 610/670 Micro Infrared Spectrometer. Ion concentrations of the tested solutions were measured by an iCAPTM QC Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer ICP-MS. Infrared (IR) thermal images and temperature changes were recorded by FLIR-T420 infrared camera. Fluorescence confocal images were obtained using Two-photon Laser Confocal Microscope (LSM 880NLO, Germany).

The water evaporation experiment was performed in laboratory with an ambient temperature of 25 °C and a humidity of 45 % without air flow. The solar simulator (CEX-HXF300) was used to output a simulated solar flux of 1 kW m−2 (1 sun), and the irradiation intensity was measured by a photoradiometer (FZ-A). The container used for evaporation is a quartz cell with a diameter of about 1 cm and a height of about 3 cm. The PNCNF foam was fabricated into small pieces with a surface area of 1 cm2 with thickness of 0.2 cm to obtain accurate power input. It floated on the simulated seawater and the container was placed in a glass beaker. Simulated seawater with a concentration of 3.5 wt % was prepared by fully dissolving NaCl(14.43 g), MgCl2(1.36 g), KCl(1.13 g), and CaCl2(0.58 g) in 500 mL of deionized water. The mass change of seawater was recorded on an electronic balance (resolution of 0.1 mg), and the temperature change was monitored using the IR camera. Measure all evaporation rates after stabilizing under 1 sunlight for 60 min.

The solar-to-vapor energy efficiency (η) was calculated according to the following equation [25]:
η =
m
ev
h
LV
/
p
in
h
LV
=
H
LV
+ Q
H
LV
=
1.91846 ×
10
6
[
T 2
/
(
T 2
- 33.91 )
]
2
Q = C (
T 2
-
T 1
)
where m ev is the mass flux per unit time (the mass of dark field evaporation is subtracted), h LV is the total enthalpy of the liquid–vapor phase change, including sensible heat (Q, J/kg) and enthalpy of the phase change (H LV, J/kg), pin is the incident solar power on the evaporator surface, T 1 (K) is the initial temperature of the water, T 2 (K) is the temperature of vaporization, and C is the specific heat of the water (4.2 kJ/(kg·K)).

The simulated seawater used in our experiment has a concentration of 3.5 wt %. It was prepared by fully dissolving NaCl (14.43 g), MgCl2 (1.36 g), KCl (1.13 g), and CaCl2 (0.58 g) in 500 mL of deionized water to obtain a 3.5 wt % simulated seawater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NGQDs and CFs
ce:section.ce:para: The amino-functionalized GQDs (NGQDs) were synthesized via a bottom-up method according to the literature [26]. After purification, a colloid of light-blue fluorescent NGQDs was yielded with uniform size of 6.3 nm in diameter (Fig. 1 a and b). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) shows that the NGQDs are crystals with a spacing of 0.24 nm corresponding to that of graphene (1120) planes [27]. Self-assembly of such NGQDs yields carbon fibers (CFs) with length ranges from several tens to hundreds of nanometers and an average diameter of 60 nm (Fig. 1c–e). HRTEM image further reveals that the nanofiber consists of interconnected crystal and amorphous domains with a similar lattice spacing of 0.24 nm to that of NGQDs, and a distance of 0.35 nm between the graphene layers, which is larger than the (002) d-spacing of conventional graphite (0.336 nm) [28]. Scanning TEM (STEM) coupled with EDX elemental mapping (Fig. 1g) displays the uniform distribution of C, N and O elements in the fiber. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns reveal that there is a nearly identical (002) layer spacing (3.48 Å) in the CFs with broadening (002) reflection peak (26.3°) compared with NGQD powders (26.1°, Fig. S1), consistent with the HRTEM results. Such a positive shift of (002) peak suggests that the self-assembly of NGQDs into CFs might induce smaller layer spacing than the loosely dispersed NGQDs powder, which is indicative of an intact graphene plane packing in the CFs.

To further investigate the chemical state of CFs, we conducted X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum gives C, N and O with content of 69.99 at % 8.79 at %, and 21.22 at %, respectively (Fig. 2 a). High-resolution C 1s spectrum shows the presence of CC, CN, and CO located at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b) [26,28]. The deconvolved peaks in N 1s spectrum can be attributed to NH2, NC, and NOx at 399.7 eV, 401.3 eV, and 406.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c) [26,28], with atomic percentages of 65.8 at %, 24.5 at % and 9.7 at %, respectively. The larger atomic percentage of NH2 compared with that of NC and NOx indicates the peripheral bonding of NH2 in NGQDs rather than doping into the backbones. O 1s spectrum possesses two peaks located at 531.0 eV and 532.6 eV corresponding to OH and NOx (Fig. 2d) [26], indicating the coexistence of oxygen-contained functional groups on the edge of NGQDs. Raman spectrum of CFs exhibits a D peak at ∼1372.7 cm−1 and a G peak at ∼1589.4 cm−1 (Fig. 2e) [28]. The intensity ratio I D/I G is widely used to reveal structural defects. The CFs show a similar I D/I G ratio (0.95) with that of NGQDs (Fig. 2e), indicating NGQDs and CFs share similar structure characteristics.

To study the self-assembly mechanism of the NGQDs into CFs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) tests were conducted to monitor the characteristic peaks of the assemblies. There are CC vibration at 1590 cm−1, COH at 1270 cm−1, NH vibration at 1620 and 3200 cm−1, and OH vibration 3400 cm−1 for both NGQDs and CFs [26], while the locations of NH vibration (broad peak for stretching 3200 cm−1) and OH stretching band (3600 cm−1) shift to higher wavelength with broadness in CFs (Fig. 2f). These shifts reflect the existence of both NH2 and OH groups engaging in hydrogen bonds along the very edge of NGQDs to drive the assembly of NGQDs into CFs [29].

Furthermore, the self-assembly behavior of NGQDs in water at different stages were captured using fluorescence confocal imaging and atomic force microscope (AFM). As shown in Fig. 3 b. The fresh NGQDs dispersion exhibits a uniform green fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of 488 nm (optimized value of NGQDs as shown in Fig. S2) with ca. 1 nm thickness (Fig. 3e). After 36 h, clear assembly occurred with the formation of green fluorescent, small clusters of several tens of nm in lateral size and 6 nm in thickness (Fig. 3c, f). As the self-assembly proceeded (72 h), much longer fibrous structure extending to several micrometers with diameters ranging from tens of nm to a few hundred nm emerged, but maintains the similar thickness (Fig. 3d, g). It is worthwhile to note that along with the self-assembly process, the fluorescence tends to concentrate on the nanofiber while that diminishes in aqueous solution, until a negligible fluorescence change in aqueous media indicates the accomplish of the self-assembly process.

Based on these findings, it could be deduced that the hydrogen bonding produced by the peripheral functional groups of adjacent NGQDs leads to the shoulder-by-shoulder alignment of NGQDs, which dominates the initial assembly stage. It is recognized that the effective range of π–π interactions was around 0.38 nm [30], which drives the π-stacking of the sp2 plane in the vertical direction, leading to the formation of fibers at the subsequent assembly process. These two competitive interactions, hydrogen bonding and π-stacking, cooperate on the self-assembly of NGQDs into fibers. These observations are consistent with our recent studies [31].

We also synthesize carboxyl and hydroxyl groups-functionalized GQDs and investigate their self-assembly behavior. Carboxyl-functionalized GQDs (CGQDs) were synthesized via a conventional top-down method, using nitric acid for the cut-off of carbon black, which possess rich carboxyl groups on the edge of GQDs (Fig. S3a, b). The synthesis of hydroxyl group-functionalized GQDs (HGQDs) involved the synthesis of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and the subsequent fusion in alkaline media (Fig. S3d, e). However, there were quite different assemblies following the similar self-assembly procedure to that of NGQDs. Only irregular aggregates that consist of huge particles with random plates were observed after self-assembly (Fig. S3c and g). It can be deduced that the carboxyl groups of CGQDs are rotated out of the sp2 plane with dihedral angles, which could form intermolecular hydrogen bonds with another CGQD (OH⋯OC). The large configuration of carboxyl groups might enlarge the intermolecular distance between CGQDs over 0.38 nm, thus weaken the π–π stacking. For HGQDs, the interactions between hydroxyl groups might be not as strong as intermolecular π–π stacking, leading to the fast aggregation of HGQDs (Fig. S3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NCFs and PNCFs
ce:section.ce:para: Annealing such CFs under Ar at 400 °C yields nitrogen-doped CFs (NCFs). It is found that the NCFs maintain the fiber-like morphology of CFs, with a slight shrink in diameters (ca. 50 nm) (Fig. S4a–c). The HRTEM indicates that the d-spacing increased to 0.46 nm after calcination (Fig. S4d), which is consistent with the negative shift of the (002) peak as shown in XRD (Fig. S4f), reflecting an enlarged interplanar distance. The much sharper diffraction peak of (002) for NCFs compared with that of CFs could be explained from the calcination induced significantly reduced amorphous domains between the neighboring regions of NGQDs connected by hydrogen bonding [31]. Moreover, NCFs also show a relatively lower I D/I G ratio (0.899). The decreased structural distortion is likely due to a high degree of graphitization with fewer defects, consisting of the HRTEM and XRD characterizations. XPS analysis shows that the atomic percentage of C and O decreases while that of N increases, revealing the consumption of C and O to form CO/CO2 during calcination (Fig. S5). Most importantly, the content of CN increases in both C 1s and N 1s spectra, suggesting the doping of nitrogen in carbon skeleton during annealing. Varying the annealing temperature results the formation of nanofibers at 300 and 500 °C (Fig. S6).

To introduce more heteroatoms in the CFs, phosphidation of the CFs was performed which led the formation of P, N-doped carbon fibers (PNCFs). PNCFs maintained fibril morphology with a slight shrink in the diameter (∼50 nm), as shown in Fig. 4 a–c. HRTEM image suggests that the lattice fringe of individual NGQDs (0.24 nm) disappears with the appearance of an interplanar spacing of up to 0.49 nm (Fig. 4d), which is among the reported largest graphene interlayer spacings. Scanning TEM (STEM) coupled with EDX elemental mapping reveal a homogeneous elemental distribution of C, N, O, and P among the fiber (Fig. 4e), manifesting the successful incorporation of N and P in carbon nanofibers. Furthermore, the more negative and sharper (002) peak also suggests that the phosphidation-induced phosphorus doping into the graphene skeleton further enlarges the interlayer spacing compared with that of NCFs (Fig. 4f) [32]. This phosphorus-doping-induced significantly enlarged interlayer spacing could benefit a reduced energy barrier for H2O molecule intercalation [14,33]. It is worthwhile to note that phosphorus doping could induce crystallinity in carbons, which is consistent with the literature [14]. Such structure reconstruction induced many interconnected amorphous and crystal domains to form discontinuous lattices, providing rich active sites for photo-to-thermal conversion. Fig. 4g shows the Raman spectrum of PNCFs, with a much lower I D/I G ratio (0.74) in comparison with that of CFs and NCFs, indicative of a higher degree of graphitization due to the removal of oxygen-included groups (e.g. OH) by P-replacement. Varying the temperature of phosphidation results the synthesis of PNCFs-300 and PNCFs-500 nanofibers (Fig. S7).

P-doping induced structural reconstruction was further analyzed by XPS. As Fig. S8a shows, the XPS survey spectrum gives signals of C, N, O and P with content of 68.92 at %,7.62 at %,19.37 at % and 4.09 at %, respectively, indicating the phosphidation possesses negligible effect on the N element while decrease the content of O, suggesting the removal of oxygen-contained groups (Table S1). Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum shows the presence of CC, CN, CO and CP located at 284.7, 285.4, 286.8 and 288.7 eV, respectively (Fig. S8b). The deconvolved peaks in N 1s spectrum can be attributed to NH2, NC at 399.7 eV, and 400.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S10c), while the absence of NOx also indicates removal of oxygen during phosphidation. There are P-C and P-O peaks located at 133.5 eV and 134.3 eV in Fig. S8d, revealing the doping of P into carbon backbone. These observations reveal that the edge sites of PNCFs are mainly functionalized with NH2 groups with P-doping in the carbon backbone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal seawater evaporation performance of CFs-, NCFs-, and PNCFs-based foams
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the solar absorber performance of as prepared CFs, NCFs, and PNCFs, the fibers were further manufactured into a self-supported 3D foam configuration via freeze-drying with adjustable size and configuration (Fig. 5 a). The CFs, NCFs and PNCFs foams were fabricated with disk-shaped and a diameter of 3 cm and a height of 5 nm for the solar evaporation test (Fig. 5b). As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the home-made steam-generation device herein is composed of the self-floating cylindrical PNCFs foam as the solar absorber, which avoids the use of a conventional thermal insulating layer that greatly simplifies the fabrication process of the solar steam desalination with reduced cost. This black PNCFs foam has a high specific surface area with micro- and mesoporous structure (Fig. S9), which improves the water transfer capability in the foam. We further measured the wettability of the PNCFs foam, which exhibits a smaller water contact angle followed by a fast water absorption as shown in Fig. 5c, suggesting its excellent hydrophilicity. The wetting ability and the continuous water pathway supplied from the primary micro-channel structure in the cross-linked fibrous skeleton enable rapid water supply and efficient evaporation during solar desalinization. The wettability of the foams of CFs and NCFs was also investigated, and both of them exhibited larger water contact angles compared to PCNFs (Fig. S10).

PNCFs foam shows an optical absorption spectrum with a strong absorption spread the entire solar spectrum (Fig. 5d), higher than those of the NCFs and CFs foams. The solar-thermal properties of the PNCFs foam was systematically investigated. IR thermal imaging-based temperature profile shows (Fig. 5e) that the surface temperature of CFs, NCFs, and PNCFs foam varied under one sun illumination (1 kW m−2). Attractively, the surface temperature of all samples increased sharply upon light irradiation, indicating good photothermal responses. A stable temperature of 51.7 °C was achieved within 1200 s for PNCFs foam, whereas the corresponding temperature of CFs and NCFs was only 40.3 °C and 44.7 °C (Fig. 5e). The temperature change of bulk water was also measured as a counterpart, which exhibited a slight increase. Typical solar-thermal evaporation curves of these samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 5f, the evaporation rates of simulated seawater, CFs, NCFs under illumination, and PNCFs under both illuminated and dark conditions were tested. Notably, the evaporation rate of seawater is measured to be 0.12 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun illumination, and the evaporation rate of PNCFs is 0.14 kg m−2 h−1 under dark conditions. PNCFs foam shows a mass change as a function of time under solar illumination of 1 kW cm−2. Therefore, the calculated water evaporation rates (Fig. 5f) of PNCFs foam was evaluated to be 1.5 kg m−2 h−1, larger than that of NCFs (1.14 kg m−2 h−1) and CFs foam (0.84 kg m−2 h−1). Furthermore, the solar-vapor conversion efficiency of the PNCFs foam was calculated to be 93.92 %, which is amongst the highest of all solar absorbers (Fig. 5g) [9,14,34–41], and even larger than that of some metal-based solar absorbers (Table S2). The thickness of the solar absorber is an essential parameter to the solar-to-heat performance. PNCFs foam with varied thickness (0.1 cm and 0.2 cm) exhibited higher solar-vapor conversion efficiency compared with those of CFs (Fig. S11). To demonstrate the feasibility of the PNCFs foam as a solar absorber for practical seawater desalination, artificial seawater was employed as the saline solution. Inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP) was employed to monitor the concentration change of four ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+) during the desalination. After solar desalination under one sun using PNCFs foam, the concentrations of all the four kinds of ions decreased by three to four orders of magnitude, which meet the requirement from the safe salinity level standards proposed by the World Health Organization (WHO), as shown in Fig. 5h. To evaluate the stability and durability of the PNCFs foams, repeated and continuous solar water evaporation tests on the same PNCFs foam was conducted. After 10 cycles, PNCFs foam exhibits the same evaporation rate as that in the first cycle, while CFs and NCFs foam show obvious decrease (Fig. 5i). The PNCFs foam was floating on hot water at 90 °C for 30 min and then subjected under ultrasonic treatment for another 30 min, which was observed to maintain its mechanical and structural stability (Fig. S12a, b). It could preserve a foam like morphology under pH = 12 (0.01 mol/L NaOH) or pH = 2 (0.01 mol/L HCl) conditions (Fig. S12c, d). Such exceptional structural stability could be attributed to the CP covalent bonds in PNCFs to prevent disintegration. The improved stability can be attributed to the incorporation of P into CFs form the covalent bond of CP, which acts as rivet between the loosely connected NGQDs in PNCFs to prevent structure collapse. The solar steam generation and desalination performances were subsequently investigated. Such an 3D foam with a highly porous structure can improve its water uptake kinetics, mainly attributing to the open and macroporous structure effectively shortens the diffusion length from the surface to the bulk, as well as the interconnected network improve the dispersion of solar-absorption and transformation active sites for accessible water capturing, making all the active sites accessible during solar desalination.

Based on the observation that the solar-to-vaper performance of the materials follows the order of PNCFs > NCFs > CFs (Fig. 4f), we rationalize a possible mechanism for the improved photothermal conversion efficiency. Heteroatoms (N, P) doping gradually creates a series of new absorption bandgaps [42], resulting in a series of energy transitions between bandgaps and continuous pathway to nonradiative recombination (Fig. 5j). Meanwhile, both N and P doping could induce structure reconstruction to form interconnected crystal and amorphous domains in PNCFs, in which the enlarged sp2 domains facilitated hopping of excitons to nonradiative recombination centers which can avoid the loss of excitons or phonons by minimized scattering [43], while the adjacent amorphous domains enable the localization of converted heat into small region without heating the water. The outdoor seawater desalination experiments were carried out using the PNCFs with a diameter of 2 cm in a home-made solar evaporation device (Fig. S13a). The outdoor light intensity and evaporation rate were monitored per hour during a day. It is found that such PNCFs-based evaporator delivers the highest hourly evaporate rate of 0.896 kg m−2 h−1 and an average daily evaporation rate of 0.644 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. S13b). Compared with the indoor experiments, the declined evaporation rate could be assigned to the lower solar irradiation intensity outdoors.

In order to investigate the salt rejection capability of the PNCFs-based evaporator, desalination tests with a wide range of salt concentration from 3.5 wt % to 15 wt % were conducted. Fig. S14a shows that the PNCFs maintained their high evaporation rate around 1.36 kg m−2 h−1 only with a slight decay at 15 wt % high salt concentration. Moreover, there is no salt crystallization on the surface of PNCFs during the 8 h solar evaporation period (Fig. S14b). Such an excellent salt tolerance could be assigned to the interconnected and hierarchical porous structure in PNCFs, which prevents salt accumulation and effectively inhibits salt crystallization during evaporation. To further determine the salt tolerance, 0.25 g of salt was loaded onto the top of the surface of the PNCFs evaporator floating on a 20 wt % NaCl solution, and the salt crystals gradually dissipated on the top of the surface after the evaporator was exposed to one sunlight for 30 min (Fig. S15). This fast desalting capability addresses the challenge that salt can grow on the top surface of the evaporator.

In summary, we developed a self-assembly strategy for the synthesis graphene quantum dots-based high-aspect-ratio carbon fibers driven by hydrogen bonding and π-stackings. Of further interest, the nanofibers were converted N, P-doped carbon for solar desalination, manipulating a self-floating cylindrical foam with a high photo-to-thermal efficiency among the best all-carbon-based solar absorbers. Such superior photothermal performance could be assigned to the enhanced light absorption, interconnected crystal and amorphous domains in the carbon, with inbuilt hierarchical porous system. This work opens a new avenue for carbon materials design and application based on the molecularly engineered GQDs assemblies.

Tengfei Jiang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Chun Guo: Methodology, Data curation. Sheng Qian: Investigation, Validation. Junhua Wang: Data curation, Visualization. Kun Zhu: Methodology. Huaiguo Xue: Supervision, Resources. Jingqi Tian: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22102140, the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20211602), the Qing Lan Project of Yangzhou University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2025.133378.
